{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.6, "R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 1.0, "R1.4": 0.5, "R1.5": 0.4, "R1.6": 0.8, "R1.7": 0.2}}

Summary: I flag a load-bearing evidence-claim mismatch (factual RMSE cannot license HTE conclusions), trim two overclaims ("scaling laws," "unmeasured confounding"), and provide a tightened rewrite that aligns the framing with what was actually measured.

## Issues to fix before this abstract is defensible

1. **The headline claim is not supported by the reported metric.** The abstract sets up "heterogeneous treatment effects" as the estimand, then reports only factual RMSE on (X, T, Y) test triples. Factual RMSE measures conditional outcome prediction on the observed treatment arm — it is consistent with arbitrarily large bias in CATE/ATE because the counterfactual arm is never scored. To support HTE claims you need PEHE (√E[(τ̂(x) − τ(x))²]), ATE bias, or policy regret. Since the benchmarks are semi-synthetic, counterfactuals are available — report them. If you keep factual RMSE, downgrade every "treatment effect" claim to "outcome prediction."

2. **"Scaling laws" is an overclaim.** Five K values on three datasets, with no functional form fit, no extrapolation test, and no theoretical motivation, is a sweep, not a scaling law. Either fit and report a parametric law (e.g., RMSE(K) ≈ a + b·K^−α) with confidence intervals, or call it a "scaling sweep" / "sensitivity to K."

3. **Framing conflates measured and unmeasured confounding.** LLM extraction from notes recovers *measured-but-unstructured* confounders — it cannot address unmeasured confounding by construction. The closing sentence ("unmeasured confounding is a persistent concern") implies the opposite. Rephrase to "confounders that exist in unstructured text but are not present in structured EHR fields."

4. **The voting mechanism is under-specified for an abstract.** "Majority-votes per-confounder annotations" leaves open whether voting is per-(document, confounder) cell across K samples, whether ties are broken, and whether confidence-weighted voting was considered. One added clause fixes this.

5. **The ablation is described but not quantified.** Saying it "confirms" the gains comes from voting, without a number, is weaker than naming the effect. Add the random-selection RMSE alongside the K=1 and K=20 figures on at least one dataset.

6. **Missing baselines.** The only comparator is the authors' own K=1. A reader cannot tell whether CausalVote beats (a) self-consistency / chain-of-thought-style aggregation, (b) using LLM confidence/logits instead of voting, or (c) ensembling outcome regressions rather than annotations. At minimum, name what is *not* being compared against and why.

7. **"Compound inference framework" is vacuous.** Replace with a concrete description: "a two-stage estimator that aggregates K LLM confounder annotations by per-feature majority vote and then fits a doubly robust outcome regression."

8. **Numeric reporting.** K=40 results are collected but not reported in the abstract — readers will assume returns saturate or reverse. State the trajectory at K=40, or say explicitly that gains plateau.

## Suggested rewrite

> We introduce CausalVote, a two-stage estimator for treatment-effect inference over observational text corpora: K large-language-model (LLM) annotations of each candidate confounder are aggregated by per-feature majority vote, and the resulting structured covariates are passed to a doubly robust outcome regression. The setting is motivated by clinical and policy pipelines in which confounders live in unstructured notes and must be extracted by repeated LLM querying before downstream causal estimation.
>
> We evaluate on three semi-synthetic benchmarks — MIMIC-III discharge notes, Yelp business reviews, and IMDB reviews — each augmented with a known treatment-assignment and potential-outcome process, so that PEHE and ATE bias can be computed against ground truth. We sweep K ∈ {1, 5, 10, 20, 40} and report PEHE, ATE bias, and factual RMSE on held-out (X, T, Y) folds.
>
> Aggregation reduces factual RMSE from 0.181 to 0.142 on MIMIC-III (K=1 → K=20, 21.5% relative), from 0.214 to 0.169 on Yelp, and from 0.252 to 0.198 on IMDB; PEHE and ATE bias track the same trend [insert numbers]. Gains attenuate between K=20 and K=40 [insert K=40 numbers]. An ablation replacing the vote with a single random sample from the K annotations recovers only [X]% of the improvement, isolating the contribution of aggregation from that of repeated sampling.
>
> We do not claim a scaling law: the K-sweep is empirical and dataset-specific. We also do not address unmeasured confounding — CausalVote targets confounders that are present in text but absent from structured fields. Within that scope, the results suggest that modest aggregation of LLM annotations (K ≈ 10–20) is a cheap and effective lever for causal inference from text.

## Unverified

I have not checked whether the cited datasets (MIMIC-III, Yelp, IMDB) have established semi-synthetic causal benchmark protocols you should align with (e.g., IHDP-style conventions); if a standard protocol exists in the HTE-from-text literature, cite it. The bracketed `[X]%` and `[insert numbers]` placeholders depend on results not present in the artifact.
